topic: Parsing - Writing a JSON Parser from Scratch

turns.content: Hey, I know you're the go-to person for this. I've read about writing a JSON parser from scratch using recursive descent but it's still fuzzy in my head. How do I actually start building one in Rust without relying on serde_json?

Welcome aboard! Don't worry, the first step is just setting up a simple tokenizer that breaks the input string into tokens like strings, numbers, and operators. In Rust, you'll likely use a String or Vec<char> to hold your token stream, then write separate recursive descent functions for each JSON structure type like objects, arrays, and primitive values.
Okay, so I need a tokenizer first. What exactly does that function look like? Does it return something specific?

Your tokenizer should iterate over the input bytes or characters and yield tokens as they appear. A good Rust type for this might be an enum with variants like Token::String, Token::Number, Token::{open_brace}, etc., so your recursive descent functions can pattern match on these tokens instead of parsing raw characters directly.
Got it. So I'm building a custom enum for tokens and then calling the tokenizer once to get that stream? And how do I handle Unicode escapes like \u0041 without using serde_json?

Exactly, you'll call your tokenizer function on the full input string first. For Unicode escapes, add a special case in your tokenizer that detects the backslash-u pattern and converts those hex characters into actual UTF-8 code points using Rust's char::from_u32 or std::str::parse_utf8 methods before yielding the resulting character token.
I see. Now I need to write the recursive descent parser itself. What's the best way to structure this in Rust to avoid stack overflows or other common mistakes?

Keep your main parse function as a single entry point that calls helper functions for objects, arrays, and primitives. You can define each parser as a separate free function or a trait implementation if you want to reuse logic across different JSON formats. Using an enum for tokens makes pattern matching cleaner than raw string comparisons.
One last thing: how do I track error positions so that when there's a syntax error, the user gets a helpful message like 'line 5, column 12' instead of just 'parse error'?

You'll maintain a Position struct with line and column fields updated as your tokenizer advances through the input. Each time you encounter an error in your recursive descent functions, capture that position into your Result or custom Error type so it can be formatted into human-readable messages later.

topic: Parsing - Writing a JSON Parser from Scratch

turns.content: I'm trying to build my own JSON parser in Rust for a side project but I keep getting stuck on the tokenizer. My current approach just reads bytes and throws errors when it hits invalid characters, which feels very fragile.

That is a common trap for beginners who try to implement parsing before building a proper lexer first. Instead of throwing errors immediately, you should separate your concerns by defining a Tokenizer trait that yields tokens like string literals, numbers, and booleans as distinct types. Once you have the tokenizer yielding these tokens, you can implement a recursive descent parser that expects specific token sequences for each JSON value type.

I see that distinction but I'm confused about how to handle unicode escapes inside strings without writing out all the escape logic manually in my recursive functions. Is it better to put all regex or pattern matching logic into the tokenizer itself so the parser just passes tokens along?

You are correct that moving the complex encoding logic into the tokenizer is the right approach, but be careful not to over-engineer your string handling by relying on external crates like unicode-normalization before you have a basic token stream. Instead of using regex for everything, consider writing explicit state machines in your Tokenizer implementation where it encounters an opening quote and iterates character-by-character looking specifically for backslash-escape sequences.

Wait, I thought the parser was supposed to do the heavy lifting with recursive descent so why am I still doing all this manual iteration logic inside my tokenizer? Isn't that what a parser should handle?

You have hit exactly the wrong assumption here: the parser is responsible for structure and grammar validation while the tokenizer handles raw data transformation. Your recursive descent functions should only check if the next token matches what the grammar expects, not parse escape sequences themselves. If you try to do both in one function, your code becomes unmaintainable because you are mixing lexical analysis with syntactic parsing.

Okay I get it but my tokenizer is still failing on invalid UTF-8 bytes when reading from a file and then trying to convert the byte slice into a Vec<u8> which loses information about where the error occurred. How do I handle these errors without losing the exact position?

You are right that you cannot lose the position information by converting blindly; instead of converting immediately, your tokenizer should return an Option<Result<Token, Error>> paired with a LineNumber and Column number for every token it yields. This way when your recursive descent parser encounters an unexpected token, you can propagate that exact error location back through the call stack to give precise diagnostics.

That sounds complicated but I'm still worried about how my custom Error type will interact with serde_json errors from other crates later on if I need to validate against standard schemas. Should I just use std::io::Error or something simpler?

For a beginner project, start by defining your own error struct that wraps the line and column information rather than relying on std::io::Error immediately; once you have a working tokenizer and parser with proper error reporting, you can later adapt that Error type to implement traits like From<serde_json::Error> for easier integration if needed. For now focus on getting the token stream correct before worrying about external crate compatibility.
